content:
# Leviticus Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 65r–65v | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 97–98

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron: **2** "When someone has a swelling, a scab, or a shiny spot on their skin, and it may be a case of skin disease, they shall be brought to Aaron the priest or to one of his sons who serves as a priest. **3** The priest shall examine the sore on the skin. If the hair in the affected area has turned white and the sore appears to go deeper than the surrounding skin, it is a case of skin disease. When the priest has examined it, he shall declare the person unclean.

**4** "But if the shiny spot on the skin is white and does not appear to go deeper than the surrounding skin, and the hair in it has not turned white, the priest shall isolate the affected person for seven days. **5** On the seventh day the priest shall examine them again. If, in his judgment, the sore looks the same and has not spread on the skin, the priest shall isolate them for another seven days. **6** On the seventh day the priest shall examine them a second time. If the sore has faded and has not spread on the skin, the priest shall declare them clean; it is only a scab. The person shall wash their clothes and be clean.

**7** "But if the scab spreads further on the skin after the person has shown themselves to the priest for their purification, they shall appear before the priest again. **8** The priest shall examine them, and if the scab has spread on the skin, the priest shall declare them unclean; it is a skin disease.

**9** "When someone has a skin disease, they shall be brought to the priest. **10** The priest shall examine them, and if there is a white swelling on the skin and it has turned the hair white, with raw flesh showing in the swelling, **11** it is a chronic skin disease on their body. The priest shall declare them unclean; he need not isolate them, for they are already unclean.

**12** "If the skin disease breaks out all over the skin, and as far as the priest can see it covers the person from head to foot, **13** the priest shall examine them, and if the skin disease has covered their whole body, he shall declare the afflicted person clean. Since it has all turned white, they are clean. **14** But whenever raw flesh appears in them, they will be unclean. **15** When the priest sees the raw flesh, he shall declare them unclean. The raw flesh is unclean; it is a skin disease. **16** But if the raw flesh changes back to white, the person shall come to the priest, **17** and the priest shall examine them again. If the sore has turned white, the priest shall declare the afflicted person clean — they are clean.

**18** "When someone has a boil on their skin that heals, **19** and in the place where the boil was there appears a white swelling or a reddish-white shiny spot, they shall show themselves to the priest. **20** The priest shall examine it, and if it appears to be deeper than the surrounding skin and the hair in it has turned white, the priest shall declare them unclean; it is a skin disease that has broken out in the boil. **21** But if the priest examines it and finds no white hair in it and it is not deeper than the surrounding skin and has faded, the priest shall isolate them for seven days. **22** If it is spreading on the skin, the priest shall declare them unclean; it is a disease. **23** But if the shiny spot stays in one place and has not spread, it is only a scar from the boil, and the priest shall declare them clean.

**24** "When someone's body has a burn from fire on their skin and the raw flesh of the burn develops a reddish-white or white shiny spot, **25** the priest shall examine it. If the hair in the shiny spot has turned white and it appears to be deeper than the surrounding skin, it is a skin disease that has broken out in the burn. The priest shall declare them unclean; it is a skin disease. **26** But if the priest examines it and finds no white hair in the shiny spot and it is not deeper than the surrounding skin and has faded, the priest shall isolate them for seven days. **27** On the seventh day the priest shall examine them. If it has spread further on the skin, the priest shall declare them unclean; it is a skin disease. **28** But if the shiny spot remains in one place on the skin and has not spread and has faded, it is the swelling from the burn, and the priest shall declare them clean; it is only a scar from the burn.

**29** "When a man or woman has a sore on their head or chin, **30** the priest shall examine the sore. If it appears to be deeper than the surrounding skin and the hair in it is thin and yellow, the priest shall declare them unclean; it is an itch — a skin disease of the head or chin. **31** But if when the priest examines the itch sore it does not appear to be deeper than the surrounding skin and there is no black hair in it, the priest shall isolate the person with the itch for seven days. **32** On the seventh day the priest shall examine the itch, and if it has not spread and there is no yellow hair in it and it does not appear to be deeper than the surrounding skin, **33** the person shall shave — but not shave the itchy area — and the priest shall isolate them for another seven days. **34** On the seventh day the priest shall examine the itch again. If the itch has not spread on the skin and does not appear to be deeper than the surrounding skin, the priest shall declare them clean. They shall wash their clothes and be clean. **35** But if the itch spreads further on the skin after their purification, **36** the priest shall examine them. If the itch has spread on the skin, the priest does not need to look for yellow hair; the person is unclean. **37** But if the itch has remained unchanged and black hair has grown in it, the itch has healed; the person is clean. The priest shall declare them clean.

**38** "When a man or woman has shiny white spots on their skin, **39** the priest shall examine them. If the shiny spots on their skin are dull white, it is only a rash that has broken out on the skin; they are clean.

**40** "If a man's hair falls out from his head, he is bald — but he is clean. **41** If his hair falls out from the front of his head, he has a receding hairline — but he is clean. **42** But if there is a reddish-white sore on his bald head or forehead, it is a skin disease breaking out on his head or forehead. **43** The priest shall examine it, and if the swelling of the sore on his bald head or forehead is reddish-white, like the appearance of skin disease on the body, **44** he has a skin disease on his head. He is unclean. The priest shall declare him unclean; the sore is on his head.

**45** "Anyone who has a skin disease shall wear torn clothes, let their hair hang loose, cover their upper lip, and cry out, 'Unclean! Unclean!' **46** They shall remain unclean for as long as they have the disease. They are unclean. They must live alone — outside the camp.

**47** "As for clothing — when a case of skin disease is on a garment of wool or linen, **48** whether in the woven or knitted fabric of linen or wool, or in leather or anything made of leather, **49** if the affected area in the garment or the leather, or the woven or knitted material, or any leather article, is greenish or reddish, it is a case of skin disease and shall be shown to the priest. **50** The priest shall examine the affected area and isolate it for seven days. **51** On the seventh day he shall examine the affected area. If it has spread in the garment — whether in the woven or knitted material, or in the leather, for whatever purpose the leather is used — the plague is destructive; the garment is unclean. **52** He shall burn the garment — whether the woven or knitted material of wool or linen, or anything made of leather — in which the plague is, for it is a destructive skin disease; it must be burned.

**53** "But if when the priest examines it the plague has not spread in the garment — in the woven or knitted material or in the leather article — **54** the priest shall order them to wash the item on which the plague is, and he shall isolate it for another seven days. **55** After it has been washed, the priest shall examine the affected area. If the affected area has not changed its appearance — even though it has not spread — it is unclean; you shall burn it in fire, whether the defect is on the inside or outside.

**56** "But if the priest examines it and the affected area has faded after it has been washed, he shall tear it out of the garment or leather or woven or knitted material. **57** If it appears again in the garment — in the woven or knitted material or in the leather article — it is spreading, and you shall burn in fire whatever has the affected area. **58** But if the garment — the woven or knitted material or the leather article — that you have washed loses the affected area, it shall be washed a second time, and it will be clean.

**59** This is the instruction for cases of skin disease in a garment of wool or linen, or in woven or knitted material, or in any leather article, to determine whether it is clean or unclean."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The chapter's place in the purity system — why skin disease, not leprosy.** Leviticus 13 is the longest chapter in the book of Leviticus (59 verses) and the most technical: it is a sustained diagnostic protocol for determining whether various conditions of the skin, scalp, and cloth represent genuine ritual impurity. The Hebrew word at the center of the chapter is *tzara'at* — a term that the King James Bible (1611) famously rendered "leprosy," and that rendering has shaped English Bible translation ever since. The problem is that *tzara'at* almost certainly does not refer to Hansen's disease (clinical leprosy) as we now understand it. The diagnostic criteria in Leviticus 13 — white hair, deepening below the surrounding skin surface, spreading bright spots — do not match clinical leprosy's presentation. More tellingly, Leviticus 13 describes *tzara'at* of garments and (in chapter 14) of houses, which Hansen's disease cannot affect. The word *tzara'at* appears to be a general category of ritually significant skin conditions that include vitiligo-like conditions, psoriasis, fungal infections of cloth and leather, and perhaps various dermatological presentations that the ancient world grouped together under a single ritual-purity framework. SOPHIA ruled that *tzara'at* may be rendered "skin disease" or preserved untranslated. PNEUMA has used "skin disease" throughout this chapter for maximum clarity, recognizing that the term covers a family of conditions the ancient Israelites treated as a unified ritual category, whatever their actual medical diversity.

**The structure of the chapter — six diagnostic cases, then the two consequences.** The chapter is built as a series of diagnostic protocols, each following a similar logic: (1) presentation of the case, (2) priestly examination, (3) determination of clean or unclean, (4) isolation provisions where needed. The six cases are: (A) swellings, scabs, and shiny spots — the general case (vv. 1–8); (B) the chronic case — raw flesh always present (vv. 9–17); (C) the post-boil case (vv. 18–23); (D) the burn case (vv. 24–28); (E) the itch/scall on the head or chin (vv. 29–37); (F) bright white spots (vv. 38–39); (G) baldness cases (vv. 40–44). Then the chapter pivots: (H) the consequences for the diagnosed person — the behavioral and social requirements of declared uncleanness (vv. 45–46); and (I) the garment protocol — applying the same diagnostic logic to fabric and leather (vv. 47–59). The movement from skin to scalp to cloth reflects the chapter's understanding of *tzara'at* as a condition that crosses the boundary between person and their immediate material environment.

**Verses 1–8 — the general diagnostic protocol: the role of the priest as diagnostician.** The chapter opens by placing the diagnostic function entirely in the hands of the priests. The person who has a suspicious skin condition does not diagnose themselves — they are brought (*yuva'*) to the priest. The priest looks (*ra'ah*), examines (*v'ra'ah*), and declares (*tihmeh / tihar* — declares unclean or clean). This is one of the most important structural features of the chapter: the priest is not a physician treating disease. He is a religious official pronouncing a ritual status. He does not heal; he assesses and declares. The separation between medical treatment and ritual classification is absolute. A person could have a skin condition that the priest would declare clean (e.g., the all-white case, vv. 12–13), or a condition that appears to be healing but the priest declares unclean (vv. 14–15: raw flesh visible). The priest's assessment is liturgical, not therapeutic.

**The seven-day isolation as the text's epistemological humility.** One of the chapter's most striking features is the repeated provision for a seven-day isolation when the priest cannot yet determine the status of a lesion (vv. 4–5, 21, 26, 31–33). This is the law's built-in acknowledgment that not every case is immediately clear. The priest who cannot decide after one examination is not permitted to rule either way prematurely; he isolates the person and waits. After seven days he examines again. If still unclear, he may isolate for another seven days. This procedure is the text's way of handling ambiguity: when you do not know, you wait, you watch, you look again. The seven-day period is significant — it is the creation week, the basic unit of sacred time in Israel. To wait one creation-week before rendering judgment is to give the condition time to declare itself, under the canopy of the sacred rhythm of time.

**Verses 12–13 — the paradox of total whiteness being clean.** The counterintuitive ruling of verses 12–13 is one of the chapter's theological puzzles: if the skin disease covers the whole body from head to foot, the person is clean. This is paradoxical — surely total coverage is worse than partial coverage? Rabbinic interpretation (b. Shabbat 55a; m. Negaim 8:1–3) offered a classic explanation: a person wholly white has entirely crossed the threshold of the liminal condition. Impurity in this system requires a condition that is in process — spreading, ambiguous, partially present. Something that has run its full course and produced a stable total outcome is no longer in the threshold state that impurity requires. The spreading edge is the ritual danger; the complete, stable outcome is beyond it. The raw flesh of verse 14 then returns the threat — raw flesh is exposed living material at the surface, the boundary of inside and outside breached in the most visceral way. This logical structure is not arbitrary; it reflects a consistent ancient Israelite theology of boundaries: the genuinely dangerous condition is the one at the threshold, caught between two states.

**Verses 45–46 — the social consequences: isolation, mourning, and the cry of "Unclean!"** The behavioral requirements imposed on the person declared unclean are some of the most emotionally charged lines in all of Leviticus. Torn clothing and loose hair (*paru'a*) are the standard signs of mourning in ancient Israel — worn at funerals, worn as a sign of grief (Lev 10:6; 2 Sam 15:32). The person with a declared skin disease is to dress and carry themselves as one in mourning, as one whose life has in some sense been interrupted by death's approach. The covered upper lip (*ve-al safam ya'ateh*) prevents even the breath from crossing outward into the shared social space. And the cry — *tame' tame'* ("Unclean! Unclean!") — is a required verbal warning, a self-announcement that clears the social path before the unclean person, so that no one unknowingly becomes contaminated by contact. The person becomes, in a liturgical sense, a walking announcement of their own altered state. They live outside the camp — *badad yeshev* (literally "they shall sit/dwell alone") — separated from the community as long as the condition persists. This social isolation is the chapter's most humanly painful provision, and it generated one of the most poignant moments in Jesus's ministry (Mark 1:40–45; Luke 17:12–19), where he touches the untouchable and the one separated from community is restored to it.

**The garment protocol (vv. 47–59) — tzara'at beyond the body.** The extension of *tzara'at* diagnosis to garments (and in chapter 14, to houses) is one of the features most clearly indicating that *tzara'at* is not a clinical disease in the biomedical sense but a ritual-purity category applied to anything within the Israelite world-order that presents the characteristic signs: greenish or reddish spots (*yerakrak* or *adamdam*) that spread and resist washing. The same framework — examination, isolation, re-examination, either clean or to be burned — is applied to wool, linen, leather, and woven fabric. The cloth protocol mirrors the skin protocol with remarkable structural parallelism: seven-day isolation (v. 50), re-examination (v. 51), either destruction by fire (v. 52) or washing and further isolation (vv. 53–54), final determination (vv. 55–58). The chapter closes (v. 59) with the same *zot ha-torah* formula as Leviticus 12 — "this is the instruction" — sealing the garment protocol as a formal sub-unit of the *tzara'at* legislation.

**Why does *tzara'at* require the priest? The theology of thresholds.** The deepest question Leviticus 13 raises is not dermatological but theological: why does a skin condition require priestly adjudication? Why does what happens at the surface of a human body become a matter of ritual cleanness and uncleanness requiring a liturgical declaration and potential exclusion from the community's sacred life? The answer the chapter implies — without making explicit — is that the human body is a sacred threshold. The skin is the boundary between the interior life of a person and the exterior world; what happens at that boundary is religiously charged. When the boundary is compromised — when the skin changes color, breaks down, becomes raw, or behaves in ways that suggest processes running unchecked — the person has entered an ambiguous condition that must be assessed by the one responsible for guarding the boundaries of the community's sacred space: the priest. The priest is the guardian of thresholds in Israel. He is stationed at the entrance of the tent of meeting (Lev 1:3, 3:2). He manages the transitions between clean and unclean, between sacred and common. The *tzara'at* protocol makes him also the guardian of the body's threshold — because the body, in Leviticus, is itself a kind of sanctuary. The person who is whole and clean may dwell within the camp, within the holy community. The person whose bodily boundary has been compromised in ways that *tzara'at* represents must temporarily live outside it, until the boundary is restored.

**Numbers 12 and Miriam — *tzara'at* as divine judgment.** It is worth noting that while Leviticus 13 treats *tzara'at* entirely in diagnostic-ritual terms — the chapter never suggests that a skin disease is punishment for sin — other texts in the Hebrew Bible do make that connection. In Numbers 12, Miriam is struck with *tzara'at* after speaking against Moses: her skin turns "white as snow," and she is shut out of the camp for seven days (Num 12:10–15), precisely the isolation of Leviticus 13. In 2 Kings 5, Naaman the Aramean general is healed of *tzara'at* by Elisha, and Gehazi is struck with it for his deception. In 2 Chronicles 26:19–21, King Uzziah is struck with *tzara'at* in the temple itself after presumptuously taking over priestly duties. These narrative uses of *tzara'at* as divine judgment create a theological counterpoint to the neutral-diagnostic tone of Leviticus 13. Leviticus 13 does not say that *tzara'at* is punishment; it provides the diagnostic and ritual framework for managing *tzara'at* however it arises. The narrative texts use that framework to signal the gravity of divine displeasure. Both moves are legitimate within the canon; they should not be collapsed into each other.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations — pages 97–98.** Codex Vaticanus pages 97–98 carry Leviticus 13 in the characteristic three-column uncial format. The LXX renders *tzara'at* throughout as *ἁφή* (*haphē*, "touch, stroke, plague") when referring to the condition in the abstract, and as *λέπρα* (*lepra*) for the diagnosed disease itself. The use of *lepra* is what gave rise to the English translation tradition of "leprosy" — but *lepra* in classical Greek referred broadly to any scaly or rough skin condition, not specifically to Hansen's disease. The LXX translators did not have Hansen's disease in mind; they chose the most common Greek term for a scaly, spreading skin condition in their medical vocabulary. The English "leprosy" came later, as the meaning of *lepra* narrowed in medical usage while Bible translations retained the term. Vaticanus also notably renders *badad yeshev* (verse 46, "shall sit alone") as *κατὰ μόνας καθήσεται* (*kata monas kathēsetai*, "shall sit by himself/herself alone"), underscoring the solitary isolation of the unclean person. At verse 59, the closing formula *zot torat ha-naga'* ("this is the instruction for the plague/stroke") appears in the LXX as *οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς ἁφῆς* (*houtos ho nomos tēs haphēs*, "this is the law of the stroke/touch"), using *nomos* (law) for *torah* (instruction) in the standard LXX way.

---

*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 65r–65v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 97–98 (LXX).*
